items.title: Middle East war makes ethical debate over AI use in war all too real
items.summary_auto: Artificial intelligence is being used in the war in the Middle East, although exactly how is being kept secret, and the question of who — or what — should make the decision to kill is up for debate.

items.title: How to watch the Milano-Cortina Paralympics on CBC
'We feel betrayed': Disability community says N.S. budget cuts to day programs, caregiver benefit are 'illogical'
items.summary_auto: Parents and advocates are sounding the alarm over the harm the provincial government’s proposed budget cuts will cause some of Nova Scotia’s most vulnerable.

items.title: Seniors at N.S. independent living facility outraged after housing authority ends in-house care
items.summary_auto: The Nova Scotia Provincial Housing Agency is changing how continuing care is delivered at facilities in Bridgetown, Berwick and Antigonish due to operational changes and complaints by some residents about mandatory costs, according to a spokesperson.

items.title: Seniors at independent living facility outraged after housing authority ends in-house care
items.summary_auto: The Nova Scotia Provincial Housing Agency is changing how continuing care is delivered at facilities in Bridgetown, Berwick and Antigonish due to operational changes and complaints by some residents about mandatory costs, according to a spokesperson.

items.title: 'We feel betrayed': Disability community says provincial budget 'punches down'
items.summary_auto: Parents and advocates are sounding the alarm over the harm the provincial government’s proposed budget cuts will cause some of Nova Scotia’s most vulnerable.

items.title: Indigenous ancestral remains found on Ontario property could cost this couple $319K
items.summary_auto: An Ontario couple was concerned to discover Indigenous ancestral remains on their property during renovations. But the biggest shock came when they learned a provincially mandated investigation could cost $319,000. Experts say the remains must be protected and treated with dignity — but that the current law should be updated so situations like this don't happen.

items.title: B.C. is adopting permanent daylight time. Sleep researchers say standard time is healthier
items.summary_auto: Scientists who study sleep are warning B.C. 's decision to adopt permanent daylight time will disrupt sleep patterns and have a negative effect on overall health, especially for children, for whom adequate sleep is critical for brain development.

items.title: Canadians reaching out to Spain after Trump's 'sadly familiar' threats
items.summary_auto: Some Canadians are feeling kinship with Spain and reaching out on social media after the European country was the latest victim of a tirade from U.S. President Donald Trump. "We're going to cut off all trade with Spain, we don't want anything to do with Spain," Trump told reporters at the White House on Tuesday.

items.title: Are Canada's 'red flag' gun laws working? No one can say
items.summary_auto: Red flag orders were a key part of the federal government's response to the 2020 Portapique, N.S., mass shooting. But two years after the measure became law, a CBC News investigation has determined that no one is keeping track of when, where or how often these emergency orders are being implemented.

items.title: How 1 woman is grappling with Quebec's new immigration program and its 'contradictions'
items.summary_auto: Social worker Florence Bollet Michel was recruited abroad to work in Quebec. After jumping over the pond with her family and putting in two years of work in Montreal, her future in the province is now uncertain due to changes the government made to its immigration pathway programs.

items.title: How America’s war with Iran has shattered the U.A.E.’s sense of security
items.summary_auto: For decades, the United Arab Emirates has positioned itself as an economic and cultural hub, connecting European and Asian markets. But on Saturday, just hours after the U.S. and Israel launched massive strikes against Iran that killed the country’s longtime Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, the U.A.E. came under fire like never before.

items.title: Elle-Máijá Tailfeathers returns Toronto film critics award, says support for Palestine cut from speech
items.summary_auto: Elle-Máijá Tailfeathers says she is returning her Toronto Film Critics Association Award after a portion of her acceptance speech expressing support for Palestine was cut out.

items.title: #TheMoment a (likely) meteor caused a sonic boom over Vancouver
items.summary_auto: Michael Unger, the programming director at the H.R. MacMillan Space Centre, tells The National about the moment a suspected meteor created a sonic boom and lit up Vancouver’s night sky.

items.title: A Vancouver hospital won't treat pregnant trauma patients. This surgeon says other facilities lack the right specialists, too
items.summary_auto: The medical director of trauma services at Vancouver General Hospital says the health authority's directive to divert pregnant patients is causing "horrific distress" among clinicians. The directive comes as the hospital operates without any on-call obstetricians.

items.title: In Nepal, a former rapper wants to be the next prime minister — and young voters are excited
items.summary_auto: As Nepalis head to the polls on Thursday in the first election since a Gen Z revolution last September toppled the previous government, one-time musician turned Kathmandu mayor Balendra Shah has galvanized the electorate, with many young voters hoping he will defeat politicians belonging to the South Asian country's old guard.

items.title: Man charged in Toronto police corruption probe ordered not to contact Ryan Wedding’s co-accused
items.summary_auto: Brian Da Costa, an alleged drug trafficker accused of bribing Toronto police officers in Project South, has been ordered not to contact a co-accused of alleged cocaine kingpin Ryan Wedding as part of his bail conditions.

items.title: As B.C. moves to permanent daylight time, what could that look like if Alberta follows suit?
items.summary_auto: As Alberta considers following B.C. 's lead in ending biannual clock changes, a sleep expert is advising the province to reject permanent daylight time in favour of a standard time model that better aligns with the solar cycle.

items.title: Who bombed a girls' school in Iran? A visual investigation
items.summary_auto: CBC's visual investigations team analyzes satellite images and video of the bombing of an elementary school that Iranian state TV says has killed at least 165 people.

items.title: Ecuador draws Cuba's ire after declaring its ambassador and his staff 'persona non grata'
items.summary_auto: Ecuador declared Cuba's ambassador, Basilio Antonio Gutiérrez, and his diplomatic staff "persona non grata" on Wednesday and gave them 48 hours to leave the South American country.

items.title: Canada hoping to charter flights for Canadians to leave U.A.E. in next 72 hours: Anand
items.summary_auto: Foreign Affairs Minister Anita Anand says the government is looking at getting Canadians out of the Middle East, including asking the United Arab Emirates to use its airspace for chartered evacuation flights.

items.title: Canada's former most wanted fugitive facing extradition to Quebec
items.summary_auto: Dave "Pik" Turmel faces numerous criminal charges, including drug trafficking, conspiracy and assault with a weapon causing bodily harm. He is also suspected of being linked to numerous other crimes committed in Quebec.

items.title: OpenAI CEO expressed 'horror and responsibility' over ChatGPT's ties to Tumbler Ridge, AI minister says
items.summary_auto: Artificial Intelligence Minister Evan Solomon says OpenAI's CEO has agreed to let Canadian experts into its safety office to help evaluate future threats following the mass shooting in Tumbler Ridge, B.C.

items.title: Elvis Stokjo still grieves his '98 gold medal loss. Here's how athletes can deal with disappointment
items.summary_auto: After pouring so much time and emotion into competing at the highest levels, major defeats can be crushing for elite athletes. Performance experts say people have to refocus to help deal with internal pressure and disappointment.

items.title: Quebec’s language watchdog to send undercover inspectors into businesses
items.summary_auto: For the first time in three years, the Office québécois de la langue française will send its inspectors to 7,800 restaurants, stores, and bars in and around Montreal as well as elsewhere in Quebec to assess the availability of service offered in French.

items.title: 'We have only just begun,' Hegseth says of Iran as Senate blocks effort to limit war powers
items.summary_auto: The U.S.-Israeli war against Iran widened sharply on Wednesday, with U.S. Secretary of War Pete Hegseth declaring that the United ​States was winning ​and that its ⁠military ‌could fight as long as needed.

items.title: N.S. premier says no changes planned for $300M budget cuts despite protests
items.summary_auto: Nova Scotia Premier Tim Houston says he’s not changing course. A week after his government tabled a budget with $300 million in cuts that sparked multiple protests around the province, including two this week at the legislature, Houston told reporters that he doesn’t expect any changes.

items.title: Alberta ‘excessively vulnerable’ to foreign interference, experts warn
items.summary_auto: Alberta is not ready to deal with the threat of online disinformation coming from foreign actors in a possible referendum campaign on separation this fall, according to national security experts.

items.title: Western Cuba hit by major blackout that energy official says could last 72 hours
items.summary_auto: A blackout hit the western half of Cuba on Wednesday, leaving millions of people in the capital Havana and beyond without power in the latest outage to affect an island struggling with dwindling oil reserves and a crumbling electric grid.

items.title: The World Baseball Classic starts today. Here's who is playing for Canada
items.summary_auto: Kansas City Royals third baseman Abraham Toro hit a bases-clearing double in the sixth inning as Canada rallied past the Philadelphia Phillies 5-3 on Wednesday in an exhibition game.

items.title: 'The bus spun 180 degrees' in Ont. crash that sent students to hospital
items.summary_auto: A school bus carrying two students collided with a tractor-trailer in Huron East Tuesday morning. Police say heavy fog reduced visibility and are reminding drivers to use extra caution.

items.title: Tracking deaths in Iran, wider Middle East as conflict rages on
items.summary_auto: Just days into the U.S. and Israeli strikes that targeted Iran and escalated into a broader regional conflict, more than 1,000 people have been killed — mainly in Iran, with the remaining deaths scattered across neighbouring countries in the Middle East.

items.title: Hamilton judge rules Ontario's sex offender registry is unconstitutional. What could happen next?
items.summary_auto: The decision is "significant" and could see changes to Ontario’s sex offender registry, depending on how the appeals process plays out, says the applicant's lawyer. The province says it plans to appeal.

items.title: Canadian man stuck in ICE detention centre for 4 months, awaiting deportation hearing
items.summary_auto: Since November, Edmonton-born Curtis Wright has been stuck in an ICE detainment facility in Texas. Since then, his family has been pushing for his release while his case moves through the courts.

items.title: What's the difference between a public inquiry and coroner's inquest into Tumbler Ridge?
items.summary_auto: A coroner's inquest has been called into the Tumbler Ridge mass shooting and B.C. 's public safety minister says a public inquiry is also possible. But how do the two differ and what can Canadians expect out of them?
